retrieved_docs.title: ABO Typing Discrepancies -- Function -- Group II
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with  group II discrepancies experience an unexpected reaction in the forward grouping. These discrepancies occur in patients with weakly reacting antigens due to a low antigen burden on the RBC surface, the presence of another antigen similar to AB antigens on the RBC surface reacting with the antibody, or partial deletion of antigens on the surface of cells. These antigens yield a weaker-than-expected reaction in the forward typing, which remains unmatched in the reverse typing reaction. Clinical scenarios that may lead to Group II discrepancies include:

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, MHC Class I -- Related Testing -- Serological Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Serological testing generally uses a recipient's lymphocytes obtained from their sera and incubated with anti–sera-containing antibodies against various HLA class 1 subtypes. The solution is then incubated with rabbit sera, providing a source of complement; a dye is also added to identify dead cells. This assay progresses serially with different HLA antibodies put into each well of a tray. Eliminating those wells with a positive result allows for determining the HLA type. This method provides a relatively quick and easy way to determine general subtypes of HLA's present. Still, it does not offer an in-depth analysis of the true molecular identity of the HLAs. This method is less common today because of its inability to detect small changes in HLA types that may make an immunological difference and cause a transfusion reaction. [8] [10] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Human Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC) -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: HLA typing is defined polymorphisms of class I and class II loci. [11]  Serology was the first HLA typing methodology. The microcytoxicity method detects the presence of antigen by cytotoxic cell death induced by activation of complement. In this in vitro assay, T or B lymphocytes are isolated from an individual and are incubated with serum containing HLA antibodies of a known HLA specificity obtained from multiparous women. [12] [13] The enhancements of molecular methodology eliminate the necessity of HLA typing by using serum with anti-HLA antibodies of all HLA antigens. Although the polymorphisms of the HLA are widely distributed throughout the gene, the vast majority of the polymorphisms congregate within exons 2 and 3. In the modern day, molecular methods are used to identify the specific nucleotide sequence polymorphisms distinctive of a specific HLA antigen. Subsequently, artificially synthesized commercially available DNA probes or primers are used to detect HLA polymorphisms. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Kell Blood Group System -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Antigens: The Kell system is highly polymorphic, consisting of 38 different blood group antigens. [10] [11] The Kell antigens are found on erythroid cells and progenitor myeloid cells and are also present in skeletal muscles and testes. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histocompatibility Antigen -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Major histocompatibility complex molecules are initially synthesized in and anchored to the rough endoplasmic reticulum (ER). After MHC class I chains are synthesized, they are first bound to calnexin, a membrane-bound molecule in the rough ER that stabilizes the chain until it can bind to beta2-microglobulin. After synthesis, chaperone proteins calreticulin, tapasin, and ERp57 additionally stabilize the molecule until an antigenic peptide binds. Proteins synthesized in the cytoplasm are then cleaved into peptides about 8 to 10 amino acids long and transported to the ER by ATP-dependent transporter proteins TAP1 or TAP2, where they can bind to MHC class I molecules. Any antigen that produces intracellular proteins, whether viral, bacterial, or tumor in etiology, could bind to MHC. Also, defective proteins that did not fold correctly and are digested by proteasomes, also known as defective ribosomal products (DRiPs), could bind to MHC. Tapasin is a molecule involved in stabilizing the transfer of peptides from TAP transporters to MHC molecules. Once the MHC I-peptide complex forms, it is transported to the cell surface, where it could potentially bind to a cytotoxic T cell and induce a response. In most healthy cells, if the complex contains a self-antigen, no immune response will be triggered. However, if the CD8 T cell identifies a non-self antigen, then it will trigger cell lysis. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Kell Blood Group System -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Erythroid lineage suppression: The presence of Kell antigens on erythroid precursors has been proven by suppression with anti-Kell antibodies on RBC production. These antibodies inhibit the colony-forming units and cause severe fetal anemia in the case of HDFN. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Histocompatibility Antigen -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of class MHC molecule function is similar; however, class II molecules first get transported to an endosome before binding to a peptide. In the endoplasmic reticulum, they are bound to an invariant chain that prevents them from binding to any peptides inside the ER. After binding to the invariant chain, the MHC-invariant chain complex is transported to an endosome. Most of the invariant chain is cleaved by a protease, and a small molecule named class II invariant chain peptide (CLIP) remains at the peptide-binding cleft. Meanwhile, the antigen-presenting cell, such as a dendritic cell, will phagocytize an exogenous peptide antigen, creating another endosome. The endosome containing the digested peptide will then fuse with the MHC-CLIP endosome. After fusion, preferential binding of the exogenous peptide displaces CLIP. Additionally, HLA-DM molecules help remove CLIP. After the MHC-peptide complex forms, it is transported to the cell surface. It can then bind to the T-cell receptor on a CD4 T cell, which will eventually result in antibody formation by B cells. Note that binding of the MHC-peptide complex to the T cell receptor is one of two interactions required for a successful immune response. The other is the binding of the B7 receptor on the antigen-presenting cell to the CD28 receptor on the CD4 T cell. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Pure Red Cell Aplasia -- Pathophysiology -- PRCA Associated with Lymphoproliferative Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Researchers suggest several mechanisms whereby T-cells reduce erythropoiesis. Such mechanisms may involve T-helper-cell-mediated antibody production, major histocompatibility complex- (MHC-) restricted or unrestricted red cell progenitor recognition, or MHC-unrestricted cytotoxicity. T-cells have also been shown to downregulate the inhibitory responses of natural killer cells and cause lysis of erythroid colony-forming units.

retrieved_docs.title: Pure Red Cell Aplasia -- Pathophysiology -- EPO-Associated Antibodies Causing PRCA
retrieved_docs.content: Although rhEPO and native EPO glycosylation sites are different, the antibody consistently targets the protein moiety's conformational epitopes and not those of the sugar moiety. Research has identified a few HLA types that correlate with increased immunogenicity against EPO. A study revealed that the allele frequency of HLA-DRB1*9 was 12.5% in cases as compared to 1.2% in controls ( P = 002). [33]

retrieved_docs.content: HLAs are identifiable via multiple detection methods.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, MHC Class I -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I is a diverse set of cell surface receptors expressed on all nucleated cells in the body and platelets. MHC is also known as human leukocyte antigen (HLA), of which there are 3 subtypes: HLA-A, HLA-B, and HLA-C. These molecules play a vital role in the immune system recognizing self from nonself, presenting foreign antigens to other immune cells. HLA class I alleles are extremely polymorphic among the world population, which presents issues relating to human tissue transplants. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Kidd Blood Group System -- Introduction -- Jk3 antigen:
retrieved_docs.content: ISBT symbol: Jk3 ISBT number: 009.003 High-prevalence antigen, with a frequency of nearly 100% in all populations (>99% in Polynesians) Cord blood RBCs: Jk3 is expressed on erythroblasts at a late stage of erythropoiesis

retrieved_docs.title: ABO Typing Discrepancies -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: An additional component of blood type is the Rh blood group system. The Rh antigens, located on proteins in the RBC membrane and encoded by RHD (OMIM 111690) and RHCE (OMIM 617970 ), further refine blood compatibility. The most common Rh antigens include D, C, c, E, and e. Individuals whose RBCs lack the RhD antigen are classified as Rh-negative

retrieved_docs.title: Kell Blood Group System -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The Kell blood group system is a collection of antigens found on red blood cells, erythroid cells, myeloid progenitor cells, and other tissues. These antigens are highly immunogenic, following only the ABO and Rhesus D systems in significance. The Kell system’s antigens, fully developed at birth, can provoke strong immune responses in transfusions and are implicated in hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn, as well as McLeod syndrome. The Kell antigens are detected on fetal erythrocytes as early as 10 weeks gestation, highlighting the importance of early recognition and management in pregnancy and transfusion medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: is noted for lacking an autoimmune mechanism despite its inclusion in the broader immune‑disorder framework. Thalassemia is also associated clinically with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Collectively, these entities illustrate that various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, especially those within the “other deficiency anemia” group, share immunologic associations and frequently intersect with chronic kidney disease and other immune‑mediated conditions, highlighting the need for coordinated hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ category of immune disorders/autoimmune comprises a diverse group of hematologic and rheumatologic conditions. Within this category, aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is recognized as an autoimmune-mediated failure of bone‑marrow production. Non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) is also classified as an autoimmune disorder in which the marrow does not adequately replace red blood cells. Acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) and its cold‑agglutinin subtype (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد) are both autoimmune hemolytic processes in which antibodies target red blood cells, leading to their premature destruction. In addition, inflammatory arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) is included as an autoimmune joint disease characterized by chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Together, these entities illustrate how autoimmune mechanisms can affect both blood formation and joint health, underscoring the importance of recognizing their shared immunologic basis for accurate diagnosis and coordinated patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
retrieved_docs.content: The collection “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” comprises a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is considered an autoimmune disorder, as are autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) and cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also members of this autoimmune group, illustrating the systemic nature of immune‑mediated disease. Although non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية) is listed among these entities, it does not result from autoantibody‑mediated red‑cell destruction; its inclusion reflects clinical overlap and underscores the importance of distinguishing immune from non‑immune mechanisms when evaluating hemolysis. Recognizing that these diverse conditions are linked by an underlying autoimmune process helps clinicians diagnose, monitor, and manage patients with immune‑driven pathology more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 934
retrieved_docs.content: that warrant careful ophthalmologic monitoring in patients with thalassemic disorders. Overall, the interrelationships among chronic kidney disease–related anemia, various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, thalassemia, and related immune‑mediated conditions underscore the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that address both hematologic abnormalities and their associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2241
retrieved_docs.content: Autoimmune disorders classified under اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ) encompass a diverse range of conditions that share an underlying immune‑mediated pathophysiology. فقر الدم الوبيل (warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia) is included in this group, reflecting the immune system’s attack on red blood cells leading to hemolysis. فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold agglutinin disease) is also a member, representing a similar hemolytic process that is triggered by cold temperatures. التهاب المفاصل (arthritis) falls within the same SMQ category, illustrating how autoimmune mechanisms can target synovial joints and cause chronic inflammation and pain. Finally, التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation) is part of اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ), highlighting that autoimmune activity can affect vascular structures and contribute to coronary artery disease. Recognizing these entities—warm and cold autoimmune hemolytic anemias, arthritis, and coronary artery inflammation—as members of a common autoimmune disorder group underscores their shared immunological basis and supports integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1392
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, arthritis, acquired hemolytic anemia, cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). This categorization reflects that each of these diseases involves dysregulated immune activity that targets the body’s own tissues—whether the bone marrow in aplastic anemia, synovial joints in arthritis, red blood cells in the two forms of hemolytic anemia, or the coronary arterial wall in coronary artery inflammation. Recognizing these conditions as part of a common autoimmune spectrum highlights shared pathogenic pathways and can guide clinicians toward integrated diagnostic approaches and therapeutic strategies that address underlying immune dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities. Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح.

retrieved_docs.content: patients with thalassemia may develop retinal complications characteristic of sickle‑cell disease. All of the aforementioned anemia subtypes—فقر الدم الوبيل, فقر الدم اللا تنسجي, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب, فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد, فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية—as well as التهاب المفاصل (arthritis) and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation) are listed as members of اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ), the standardized medical query for immune‑mediated or autoimmune disorders. Although فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية is defined as a non‑immune hemolytic anemia, it is still grouped within the immune‑disorder category for classification and reporting purposes, reflecting a pragmatic rather than a pathophysiological contradiction. Together, these relationships illustrate how various deficiency and hemolytic anemias are organized under a common classification, how specific anemias relate to ocular complications, and how many of these

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2475
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية” includes several distinct disorders: التهاب المفاصل, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب, فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية, and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية. This grouping reflects that both autoimmune and non‑autoimmune forms of hemolytic anemia, as well as inflammatory arthritis, are considered part of the broader spectrum of immune‑mediated diseases. Recognizing these conditions as members of the autoimmune disorder SMQ helps clinicians categorize and manage diseases that involve dysregulated immune responses.
